learning_objectives: Trace the evolution of HCI from command-line interfaces through GUIs to modern interaction paradigms
Apply Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics to evaluate existing interfaces systematically
Identify common usability problems in real-world software and explain their cognitive basis
readings: Norman, The Design of Everyday Things, Ch. 1-2
Nielsen, '10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design'
Card, Moran, & Newell, 'The Psychology of Human-Computer Interaction' (selected chapters)

lecture_notes.introduction: Human-Computer Interaction sits at the intersection of psychology, design, and engineering, studying how people interact with computing systems and how to design those systems for effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction. The history of HCI is the history of making computers accessible to progressively wider audiences: from trained operators who learned machine-specific commands, through the GUI revolution that introduced visual metaphors (desktop, windows, trash can), to today's touch, voice, and gesture interfaces that aim to make interaction natural and invisible. Understanding this history reveals a consistent pattern: each paradigm shift reduced the cognitive burden on the user by aligning the interface more closely with how humans naturally think, perceive, and act. This course teaches you to design with the user, not just for the user, by grounding every design decision in evidence about human cognition, perception, and behavior. We begin with the foundational principles that underpin all good interface design, establishing the vocabulary and analytical frameworks you will use throughout the course.
key_concepts: HCI evolution: batch processing, command-line, GUIs (Xerox PARC, Macintosh), web, mobile, voice, gesture, AR/VR
Usability defined: learnability, efficiency, memorability, error rate, and user satisfaction (ISO 9241-11)
Nielsen's 10 heuristics: visibility, real-world match, user control, consistency, error prevention, recognition over recall, flexibility, aesthetic design, error recovery, help
Norman's design principles: affordances (what actions are possible), signifiers (how users discover them), mapping (relationship between controls and effects), feedback (confirmation of actions)
Mental models: the user's internal understanding of how a system works, often divergent from the implementation model
Gulf of execution: gap between what the user wants to do and what the interface allows; minimized by visible, intuitive actions
Gulf of evaluation: gap between system state and user's understanding of that state; minimized by clear, immediate feedback
Human cognitive limitations: attention (selective and limited), working memory (7 plus or minus 2 items), decision fatigue (choice overload)
Error classification: slips (correct intention, wrong action) vs mistakes (wrong intention); different design strategies for each
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a heuristic evaluation of a popular mobile banking app: systematically walk through each of Nielsen's 10 heuristics, identify at least 8 usability violations with severity ratings (cosmetic, minor, major, catastrophic), propose specific design improvements for the top 3 violations, and compare findings across student evaluators to demonstrate inter-rater variability
setup_requirements: Figma account for prototyping (free education plan)
Screen recording software for documenting evaluations
Usability evaluation template with heuristic checklist
Mobile devices for hands-on app evaluation

review.value: The gap between what a user wants to accomplish and what the interface allows them to do. Good design minimizes this gulf by making available actions visible, matching system operations to user intentions, and providing clear affordances. A door with a flat plate affords pushing; a handle affords pulling

Users should not have to remember information from one screen to use another. Options, actions, and contextual information should be visible or easily retrievable. A dropdown menu offers recognition (see options, choose one); a command-line requires recall (remember the exact command). Recognition is always easier than recall

A heuristic evaluation by 3-5 expert evaluators identifies 60-75% of usability problems in hours rather than the days required for user testing. It is a discount usability method: cheaper and faster than user testing but finds different problems. Use heuristic evaluation for early-stage filtering, user testing for validation

Slips (user knows what to do but mis-executes): prevent with constraints (disable invalid options), undo support, and confirmation for destructive actions. Mistakes (user has wrong understanding): prevent with clear system status, match with real-world concepts, and progressive disclosure that guides understanding

learning_objectives: Apply the user-centered design process as defined by ISO 9241-210
Distinguish between user-centered, activity-centered, and technology-centered design approaches
Plan a UCD project with appropriate methods at each phase
readings: ISO 9241-210: Human-Centred Design for Interactive Systems (summary)
Gould & Lewis, 'Designing for Usability: Key Principles and What Designers Think' (1985)

lecture_notes.introduction: User-centered design is a philosophy and process that places the end user at the center of every design decision. Rather than building features based on technical capability or stakeholder assumptions and hoping users will adapt, UCD starts by understanding who the users are, what they need to accomplish, and what constraints they operate under, then designs solutions that fit naturally into their existing workflows and mental models. The UCD process, formalized in ISO 9241-210, consists of iterative cycles of understanding context of use, specifying user requirements, producing design solutions, and evaluating designs against requirements. Each cycle narrows the design space from broad possibilities to a specific, validated solution. This approach was developed in response to decades of technology-centered design that produced powerful systems nobody could use: medical devices with 200-page manuals, enterprise software requiring weeks of training, and consumer products returned in frustration. The evidence is clear that investing in understanding users before writing code produces better products faster and cheaper than fixing usability problems after launch.
key_concepts: UCD iterative cycle: understand context, specify requirements, produce design, evaluate against requirements, repeat
ISO 9241-210 principles: design based on user understanding, user involvement throughout, iterative refinement, multidisciplinary team
Design thinking: empathize, define, ideate, prototype, test as a complementary creative framework to UCD
Stakeholder analysis: identifying all parties affected by the design (primary users, secondary users, indirect stakeholders)
Context of use: who are the users, what are their tasks, what equipment do they use, and what physical and social environment do they work in
User requirements vs system requirements: what users need to accomplish vs how the system will support those needs
Participatory design: involving users as co-designers rather than just research subjects, especially for specialized domains
Lean UX: integrating UCD into agile development with rapid hypothesis testing and minimum viable designs
Design sprints: time-boxed (typically 5-day) UCD cycles for rapidly prototyping and testing design ideas
lecture_notes.demonstration: Plan a UCD project for a hospital nurse scheduling system: identify all stakeholders (nurses, charge nurses, administrators, patients), define context of use (busy ward, shared computer, interruptions), outline which UCD methods to use at each phase (contextual inquiry for understanding, personas for requirements, Crazy 8s for ideation, Figma for prototyping, think-aloud for evaluation), and create a project timeline with iteration points
setup_requirements: Project planning tool (Notion or similar)
UCD method selection matrix (course handout)
Case study materials for the nurse scheduling domain

review.value: Gould and Lewis (1985) identified three principles that remain the foundation of UCD: (1) Early focus on users and tasks through direct contact. (2) Empirical measurement of usability with prototypes and real users performing real tasks. (3) Iterative design where the cycle of design-test-redesign is repeated until usability objectives are met

Users can be involved as informants (providing data through interviews and observation), evaluators (testing prototypes and providing feedback), or co-designers (actively participating in design decisions). The appropriate level depends on user availability, domain complexity, and project constraints. More involvement generally produces better designs

Traditional UCD runs ahead of development by one sprint: researchers are in sprint N+1 while developers build sprint N. Design deliverables are lightweight (sketches, wireframes, not pixel-perfect mockups). Usability testing happens every sprint with 3-5 users. This maintains UCD rigor within agile velocity constraints

Every dollar invested in UCD during design saves $10-100 during development (avoiding building the wrong features) and $100-1000 after launch (avoiding support costs, redesigns, and lost users). The ROI of UCD is one of the most well-documented findings in software engineering

learning_objectives: Conduct user interviews and contextual inquiries that reveal genuine user needs
Synthesize research findings into personas, journey maps, and user stories
Select appropriate research methods based on project phase and research questions

lecture_notes.introduction: User research replaces assumptions with evidence. Every design team has beliefs about who their users are and what they need, but these beliefs are frequently wrong because designers are not their users. The curse of knowledge means that once you understand how a system works, you cannot imagine not understanding it, making it impossible to predict where novice users will struggle. User research methods fall into two broad categories: attitudinal (what people say) and behavioral (what people do). Interviews and surveys capture attitudes, goals, and stated preferences. Observation and contextual inquiry capture actual behavior, workarounds, and unstated needs. The most valuable insights often come from the gap between what users say they do and what they actually do. This session covers the core user research methods that every HCI practitioner must master: how to plan and conduct user interviews that elicit genuine insights rather than superficial preferences, how to observe users in context to discover latent needs, and how to synthesize research data into actionable design artifacts that keep the team aligned on user needs throughout development.
key_concepts: User interview techniques: open-ended questions, active listening, follow-up probing, avoiding leading questions
Contextual inquiry: observing users performing real tasks in their actual environment, master-apprentice model
Survey design: question types, response scales, sampling strategy, and when surveys are appropriate vs insufficient
Diary studies: participants self-report experiences over days or weeks for longitudinal behavioral data
Affinity diagramming: physically grouping research observations into clusters to identify themes and patterns
Personas: composite user profiles based on research patterns, with goals, behaviors, frustrations, and context
User journey maps: end-to-end visualization of user experience with touchpoints, emotions, and pain points at each stage
Jobs to be done: focusing on what job users are hiring the product to do rather than what features they request
Research triangulation: using multiple methods to validate findings and reduce bias from any single method
Research ethics: informed consent, privacy protection, avoiding harm, and responsible use of participant data
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a mock user interview about grocery shopping habits: demonstrate open-ended questioning technique, show how to probe deeper when the participant gives surface-level answers, synthesize findings from 3 pre-recorded interviews using affinity diagramming on a whiteboard, create a persona based on emerging patterns, and build a journey map of the grocery shopping experience highlighting pain points and design opportunities
setup_requirements: Interview guide template
Affinity diagramming supplies (sticky notes, whiteboard) or digital tool (Miro, FigJam)
Persona template with goals, behaviors, frustrations
Journey map template with stages, actions, emotions, opportunities

review.value: Users are unreliable reporters of their own future behavior. Asking 'Would you use feature X?' produces useless data because people cannot accurately predict their behavior. Instead ask: 'Tell me about the last time you tried to accomplish X. What did you do?' Past behavior is the best predictor of future behavior

Research by Nielsen and Landauer shows that 5 users find approximately 80% of usability problems. Testing with 5 users in three rounds (test, fix, retest) is more effective and cheaper than testing with 15 users once. Diminishing returns after 5 make small frequent tests optimal

A persona based on 5-8 real user interviews captures common behavioral patterns. A persona based on stakeholder assumptions or demographic data is a fictional character that does not predict real behavior. The power of a persona is proportional to the quality of the research behind it

Users do not want a quarter-inch drill; they want a quarter-inch hole. They do not even want the hole; they want to hang a picture. Understanding the job (display meaningful memories) reveals design opportunities that a feature-focused interview (what drill features do you want?) completely misses

learning_objectives: Decompose complex user tasks into hierarchical structures using HTA
Identify pain points and efficiency opportunities through task flow analysis
Map user goals to interface features through task-centered system design
readings: Lewis & Rieman, 'Task-Centered User Interface Design', Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Task analysis bridges the gap between user research (understanding who users are and what they need) and design (creating interfaces that support those needs). It provides a systematic method for decomposing what users do into a structured representation of goals, subtasks, and actions that directly informs interface design decisions. Hierarchical Task Analysis (HTA) breaks complex tasks into increasingly detailed subtasks, revealing the decision points, information needs, and error opportunities at each level. Task flow analysis maps the sequence of actions across screens and systems, exposing inefficiencies, dead ends, and unnecessary steps that can be eliminated or streamlined. The output of task analysis is not a design; it is a detailed understanding of the problem that constrains the design space. A good task analysis reveals which tasks are frequent vs rare (frequent tasks deserve prominent placement), which are simple vs complex (complex tasks need guidance), and which are critical vs casual (critical tasks demand error prevention). This session teaches you to conduct task analysis that produces actionable design insights.
key_concepts: Hierarchical Task Analysis (HTA): decomposing a goal into subtasks with plans specifying execution order and conditions
Task decomposition levels: goal (what the user wants to achieve), subtask (major steps), action (atomic UI interactions)
Plans: specifying the order and conditions for executing subtasks (do 1 then 2; do 1, if X then 2 else 3; do 1 and 2 in any order)
Task flow diagrams: visualizing the sequence of steps across screens, including decision points and error paths
Cognitive task analysis: identifying the mental operations (decisions, problem-solving, memory retrieval) required by a task
GOMS model: Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection rules for predicting task completion time and comparing designs
Fitts's Law: movement time = a + b * log2(distance/width + 1); implications for target sizing and placement
Task frequency and criticality matrix: mapping tasks by how often they are performed and how important they are to prioritize design effort
Error analysis: identifying where in the task flow errors are most likely and most costly, then designing prevention and recovery
Task scenario writing: concrete narrative descriptions of users performing tasks in context, used to test designs
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a hierarchical task analysis of booking a flight: decompose the goal into subtasks (search, select outbound, select return, choose seats, add baggage, enter passenger details, pay), create an HTA diagram with plans, map the task flow across screens, identify the highest-frequency and highest-criticality subtasks, calculate predicted task time using simplified GOMS, and propose design improvements that reduce steps for the common case
setup_requirements: Diagramming tool (Lucidchart, draw.io, or Figma)
HTA notation reference sheet
Real-world airline booking site for reference
Task scenario templates

review.value: Tasks performed daily should be one click from the home screen. Tasks performed weekly should be within two clicks. Tasks performed monthly can be in a settings menu. Burying a frequent task in a deep menu structure is a design failure that task analysis prevents

Larger and closer targets are faster to click. Make important buttons large. Place related actions near each other. Put destructive actions far from constructive ones. Screen corners and edges have infinite width (cursor stops there), making them extremely fast targets for desktop applications

Task analysis identifies where errors are likely (complex decision points, unfamiliar terminology, irreversible actions). These steps need: clear instructions, inline validation, undo capability, and confirmation dialogs. Do not distribute design effort evenly; concentrate it on error-prone steps

review.key: The Happy Path Is Not Enough
review.value: Task flow analysis must include error paths, edge cases, and alternative flows. What happens when the flight is sold out? When payment fails? When the session times out? Users spend significant time on non-happy paths, and these paths are where frustration and abandonment occur

learning_objectives: Design clear navigation structures using card sorting and tree testing methodologies
Create sitemaps and content hierarchies that match users' mental models
Apply labeling best practices that use user vocabulary rather than internal jargon

lecture_notes.introduction: Information architecture determines how content is organized, labeled, and navigated in a digital product. It is the structural design of shared information environments, the blueprint that defines where things go and how users find them. Poor information architecture is the hidden cause of many usability failures: users cannot find the feature they need because it is categorized under a label they do not understand, or they cannot complete a task because related functions are scattered across unrelated sections. The challenge is that the way a development team organizes features (by technical component, by team ownership, or by development sprint) rarely matches how users think about them. Users organize information by task, by topic, or by personal mental model, and these organizations vary across user groups. Card sorting and tree testing are the evidence-based methods for discovering how users expect information to be organized and for validating whether a proposed structure matches those expectations. This session teaches you to design information architectures that are grounded in user research rather than organizational assumptions.
key_concepts: Information architecture components: organization schemes, labeling systems, navigation systems, search systems
Organization schemes: alphabetical, chronological, topical, audience-based, task-based, and hybrid approaches
Card sorting: open sort (users create categories), closed sort (users assign items to predefined categories), hybrid approaches
Tree testing: validating navigation structure by asking users to find specific items in a text-only hierarchy (no visual design influence)
Sitemaps: visual representation of the hierarchical structure of a website or application's content and features
Navigation patterns: global navigation (persistent across all pages), local navigation (within a section), contextual navigation (related content links)
Breadcrumbs and wayfinding: showing users where they are in the hierarchy and how to navigate back
Labeling: using terminology that matches user vocabulary, not internal jargon or technical terms
Search design: search box placement, autocomplete, faceted filtering, and result page design
Polyhierarchy: allowing content to exist in multiple categories when users reasonably expect to find it in different places
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design the IA for an online learning platform: conduct an open card sort with student participants to discover how they categorize 40 content items (courses, tutorials, forums, assignments, grades, certificates), analyze sort results using similarity matrix and dendrograms, create a sitemap based on emerging categories, validate with a tree test (can users find specific items?), and iterate the structure based on tree test failure points
setup_requirements: Card sorting tool (OptimalSort or physical index cards)
Tree testing tool (Treejack or similar)
Diagramming tool for sitemaps
Content inventory spreadsheet for the learning platform

review.value: A common IA failure is organizing content by team structure: 'Engineering', 'Marketing', 'Support'. Users do not know or care about your org chart. They think in terms of tasks ('file a claim', 'track my order') or topics ('billing', 'account settings'). Card sorting reveals the user's organizational model

In tree testing, a task success rate below 70% indicates a structural problem: users cannot find the item where you placed it. Examine the paths users took to find where they expected the item to be. Moving the item (or adding a cross-link) to match user expectations often solves the problem immediately

review.key: The Three-Click Rule is a Myth
review.value: Research does not support the idea that users give up after three clicks. What matters is information scent: does each click feel like progress toward the goal? Users will happily click ten times through a clear, well-labeled path but abandon after two clicks through a confusing one

Test labels with users. 'Settings' vs 'Preferences' vs 'Options' are not interchangeable; users have strong preferences that vary by platform convention. Use the exact words users use in interviews and card sorts. Internal codenames, acronyms, and technical terms are invisible to users

learning_objectives: Create wireframes that communicate structure and interaction without visual distraction
Build interactive prototypes in Figma with linked screens, transitions, and component states
Select the appropriate prototype fidelity based on the design phase and evaluation goals
readings: Rettig, 'Prototyping for Tiny Fingers' (Communications of the ACM, 1994)

lecture_notes.introduction: Prototyping is the practice of creating representations of a design before building it, enabling rapid exploration, communication, and testing at a fraction of the cost of full development. The fidelity of a prototype, how closely it resembles the final product, should match the maturity of the design and the questions you need to answer. Paper sketches test conceptual direction and navigation flow in minutes. Wireframes test information hierarchy and interaction patterns in hours. Interactive prototypes test specific interactions and user flows in days. High-fidelity mockups test visual design and emotional response. Using a high-fidelity prototype too early wastes effort on visual polish that will change, and users fixate on aesthetic details instead of providing feedback on structure and flow. Using a low-fidelity prototype too late fails to reveal interaction-specific problems like confusing transitions or missing states. This session teaches you to create prototypes at every fidelity level with Figma as the primary tool, understanding that the purpose of a prototype is not to look like the final product but to answer specific design questions as cheaply and quickly as possible.
key_concepts: Prototype fidelity spectrum: paper sketches, wireframes, clickable wireframes, interactive prototypes, high-fidelity mockups
Wireframe conventions: grayscale, placeholder content, emphasis on layout and hierarchy, no brand styling
Figma frames: screen-level containers with constraints, auto-layout, and responsive sizing
Figma components: reusable UI elements with variants (button states, input states) for consistency and efficiency
Figma prototyping: linking frames with click interactions, transitions, overlays, scroll behaviors, and smart animate
Prototype interactions: click/tap, hover, drag, scroll, keyboard input using Figma's interaction panel
State management in prototypes: showing different component states (default, hover, active, disabled, error) on interaction
Wizard of Oz prototyping: human simulates system responses behind the scenes for complex AI or voice interactions
Design critique: structured feedback sessions focusing on specific design questions rather than general opinions
Iterative refinement: prototype-test-revise cycles that converge on a validated design through successive approximation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a complete prototype in Figma for a food delivery app: start with paper sketch wireframes, translate to Figma wireframes with auto-layout, add interactive prototyping for the order flow (browse restaurants, select items, customize order, checkout), implement component variants for button states and form validation, create a smart-animate transition for the cart sliding up, and demonstrate how to share the prototype link for remote testing
setup_requirements: Figma account (free education plan)
Wireframe component library (Figma community file)
Paper and markers for initial sketching
Screen recording for prototype walkthroughs

review.value: Concept exploration: paper sketches (minutes to create, disposable). Structure definition: wireframes (hours, focus on layout). Interaction design: clickable prototypes (days, test specific flows). Visual design: hi-fi mockups (days, test aesthetics and brand). Presenting a hi-fi prototype in week 1 anchors the team on one direction too early

review.key: Figma Components Save Time and Ensure Consistency
review.value: Define buttons, form fields, cards, and navigation elements as components with variants (primary/secondary, enabled/disabled, default/hover/active). Changes to the main component propagate to all instances. This mirrors how design systems work in code and prepares the handoff to developers

Use prototypes for interactions that are difficult to describe in words or static mockups: how a menu slides open, how a list reorders on drag, how content transitions between states. If the interaction is simple (click a button, go to a new page), a wireframe with annotations is sufficient

A prototype should be cheap enough to throw away without regret. If the team invests 40 hours in a prototype, they become attached to it and resist changes suggested by testing. Keep prototypes lightweight so that test results lead to redesign rather than rationalization

learning_objectives: Apply Gestalt principles of visual perception to organize interface elements effectively
Design with consistent typography, color systems, and spacing for visual hierarchy
Create visual designs that guide user attention to the most important elements
readings: Williams, 'The Non-Designer's Design Book', Ch. 1-8 (CRAP principles)
Lidwell et al., 'Universal Principles of Design' (selected entries)

lecture_notes.introduction: Visual design transforms a structurally sound interface into one that communicates hierarchy, guides attention, and evokes the appropriate emotional response. While wireframes and prototyping establish what is on each screen and how users interact with it, visual design determines how users perceive and interpret that content in the first fraction of a second before conscious thought engages. The Gestalt principles of visual perception, discovered by psychologists in the early 20th century, explain how the human visual system organizes elements into meaningful patterns: proximity groups related items, similarity connects them, and closure fills in gaps. Typography establishes content hierarchy through size, weight, and contrast. Color carries meaning (red for danger, green for success) and draws attention to critical elements. Spacing (white space) provides visual breathing room that prevents cognitive overload. Together, these principles create visual designs where the most important information stands out, related elements are clearly grouped, and the overall impression is one of clarity and professionalism.
key_concepts: Gestalt principles: proximity (nearby elements are grouped), similarity (similar elements are grouped), closure (mind completes incomplete shapes), continuity (eye follows smooth paths), figure-ground (elements perceived as foreground or background)
Visual hierarchy: using size, color, contrast, and position to indicate the relative importance of interface elements
Typography: type scale (heading sizes, body, caption), line height, line length (45-75 characters optimal), font pairing rules
Color systems: primary, secondary, and accent colors with semantic meaning (error red, success green, warning amber)
Color contrast: WCAG minimum 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text, ensuring readability for all users
Spacing systems: 8px grid for consistent spacing, padding, and margin that creates visual rhythm
White space: intentional empty space that reduces cognitive load and groups related content
CRAP principles: Contrast (make different things different), Repetition (reuse design elements for consistency), Alignment (align everything with something), Proximity (group related elements)
F-pattern and Z-pattern: common eye scanning patterns for content-heavy and marketing pages respectively
Emotional design: Norman's three levels of processing (visceral, behavioral, reflective) and how visual design addresses each
lecture_notes.demonstration: Redesign a poorly designed settings screen: start by identifying visual design violations (no hierarchy, inconsistent spacing, clashing colors, poor typography), apply Gestalt proximity to group related settings, establish a type scale for headings and body text, define a 3-color system with semantic meaning, implement an 8px spacing grid, add white space to reduce density, and show the before/after comparison with explanation of each design decision
setup_requirements: Figma with a design system component library
Color contrast checking tool (WebAIM or Figma plugin)
Typography scale calculator
Sample poorly designed screens for redesign exercise

review.key: Proximity Is the Strongest Grouping Cue
review.value: Items near each other are perceived as related even without borders or backgrounds. Two form fields 8px apart are clearly a group; the same fields 32px apart feel separate. Use proximity as the primary grouping mechanism and reserve borders for emphasis or separation of major sections

The most important element on the screen should have the highest visual contrast. Size difference, color saturation, weight (bold), and isolation (surrounded by white space) all create contrast. If everything is bold, nothing stands out. Hierarchy requires restraint: emphasize few things so emphasis has meaning

60% of the interface uses the dominant color (typically a neutral: white, light gray). 30% uses the secondary color (brand accent, navigation background). 10% uses the accent color (CTA buttons, highlights, alerts). This ratio creates visual balance and ensures accent colors attract attention

Every time a user encounters a new visual pattern (a different button style, unexpected spacing, novel icon), they spend cognitive effort interpreting it. Consistent visual patterns become invisible through familiarity, letting users focus on content and tasks. Inconsistency creates a subtle but persistent cognitive tax

learning_objectives: Implement common interaction design patterns for navigation, forms, search, and data display
Design micro-interactions and animations that provide feedback and enhance usability
Evaluate when to use established patterns versus innovate with novel interactions

lecture_notes.introduction: Interaction patterns are proven solutions to recurring design problems, analogous to software design patterns in engineering. Just as a developer uses the Observer pattern for event handling rather than inventing a new mechanism, a designer uses established interaction patterns for navigation, forms, search, and data display because users have already learned how these patterns work from years of using other products. A hamburger menu, a pull-to-refresh gesture, a swipe-to-delete action, a floating action button: each is an interaction pattern that carries user expectations about how it behaves. Using established patterns reduces learning time because users transfer knowledge from other products. Deviating from patterns requires the user to learn something new, which is appropriate only when the pattern genuinely does not fit the use case. Beyond these macro-level patterns, micro-interactions, the small animations and feedback that accompany user actions, play a crucial role in making interfaces feel responsive and alive. A button that depresses on tap, a toggle that animates between states, a loading indicator that communicates progress: these micro-interactions communicate system status, confirm actions, and provide the sensory feedback that makes digital interfaces feel tangible.
key_concepts: Navigation patterns: tab bar, bottom navigation, navigation drawer, breadcrumbs, wizard/stepper for sequential tasks
Form patterns: inline validation, floating labels, progressive disclosure (show fields as needed), smart defaults, autofill
Search patterns: instant search, autocomplete suggestions, recent searches, faceted filtering, search scope selection
Data display patterns: cards, data tables with sorting/filtering, infinite scroll vs pagination, master-detail
Action patterns: floating action button (primary action), contextual menus, swipe actions, long press, pull to refresh
Micro-interactions: trigger (user action), rules (what happens), feedback (what user sees), loops/modes (repetition and variation)
Animation principles: duration (200-500ms for most transitions), easing (ease-out for entering, ease-in for exiting), purpose (always functional, never decorative)
Loading patterns: skeleton screens (show layout placeholder), progress indicators (determinate and indeterminate), optimistic UI
Empty state patterns: instructional (tell user how to add content), encouraging (motivate first action), educational (teach the feature)
Error patterns: inline field errors, toast notifications for transient errors, error pages with recovery actions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design interaction patterns for a task management app: implement inline form validation with real-time feedback, build a swipe-to-complete gesture with undo toast notification, design a skeleton loading screen for the task list, create an encouraging empty state for new users with an illustration and call-to-action, animate a task card sliding into the completed section with a subtle checkmark micro-interaction, and compare this against a version without micro-interactions to demonstrate the impact on perceived quality
setup_requirements: Figma with interactive prototyping configured
Animation reference library (Material Design motion guidelines)
Interaction pattern reference (Mobbin or UI Patterns)
Lottie animation library for complex micro-interactions

review.value: Use established patterns for standard interactions (navigation, forms, search). Users have learned these patterns from thousands of hours using other apps. Innovate only when established patterns genuinely fail to support the specific use case. Novel interactions have a learning cost that must be justified by sufficient benefit

Skeleton screens (gray placeholder shapes mimicking the content layout) are perceived as faster than loading spinners because they communicate what is coming and create a sense of progress. Users perceive skeleton screen loading as 10-20% faster than equivalent spinner loading, even when the actual time is identical

Most UI animations should be 200-300ms. Faster feels jarring; slower feels sluggish. Small elements (button state changes) animate faster (100-200ms). Large elements (page transitions, modals) animate slower (250-500ms). Reduce motion for users who have enabled reduced-motion preferences in their OS settings

The empty state is often the first thing a new user sees. Instead of a blank screen or a 'no items yet' message, use it as an onboarding moment: explain the feature's value, provide a clear first action, and include visual appeal. The empty state is the difference between a user who engages and one who bounces

learning_objectives: Plan and conduct moderated usability tests with think-aloud protocol
Analyze usability test results using task metrics and qualitative coding
Prioritize findings by severity and translate them into actionable design improvements
readings: Rubin & Chisnell, 'Handbook of Usability Testing', Ch. 1-8
Nielsen Norman Group, 'How to Conduct a Usability Test'

lecture_notes.introduction: Usability testing is the gold standard for evaluating whether a design works for real users. Unlike heuristic evaluation (expert inspection) or A/B testing (quantitative optimization), usability testing provides direct behavioral evidence of how users interact with a design, where they succeed, where they struggle, and why. A moderated usability test involves a facilitator, a participant, and a set of realistic tasks. The participant attempts the tasks while thinking aloud, narrating their thoughts, expectations, and confusion in real time. The facilitator observes without helping, asking neutral follow-up questions to understand the participant's mental model. The result is rich qualitative data that reveals not just what usability problems exist but why they exist and how severe they are. This session teaches the complete usability testing process: planning the study (recruiting, writing tasks, setting up the environment), conducting the sessions (facilitating without biasing, managing think-aloud, handling difficulties), and analyzing the results (identifying patterns, rating severity, prioritizing improvements). These skills are fundamental to every UX role and applicable to any product at any stage of development.
key_concepts: Usability test types: moderated (facilitator present) vs unmoderated (remote, asynchronous), formative (find problems) vs summative (measure performance)
Participant recruitment: 5 users per round, representative of target audience, screened for relevant characteristics
Task design: realistic scenarios that do not lead the participant, specific enough to evaluate but open enough to reveal behavior
Think-aloud protocol: participants verbalize thoughts during tasks, revealing expectations, confusion, and decision-making processes
Facilitator neutrality: never helping, never leading, using neutral prompts ('What are you thinking?' not 'Try clicking that button')
Quantitative metrics: task success rate, time on task, error count, System Usability Scale (SUS) score
Qualitative analysis: noting observations, coding themes, identifying patterns across participants
Severity rating: cosmetic (fix if time), minor (low priority), major (important to fix), catastrophic (must fix before release)
Findings prioritization: severity times frequency matrix to determine which problems to fix first
Test report deliverables: executive summary, methodology, findings with evidence (video clips), recommendations with severity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a live usability test of a prototype checkout flow: brief a volunteer participant, administer the think-aloud protocol, observe task completion for three tasks (find a product, add to cart, complete checkout), handle a moment where the participant gets stuck without helping them, debrief with follow-up questions, then analyze the session identifying 5 usability findings with severity ratings and recommended fixes
setup_requirements: Figma prototype with interactive checkout flow
Screen and audio recording software (OBS or similar)
Usability test script with welcome, tasks, and debrief questions
Observation note-taking template
Volunteer participant (not from the design team)

review.value: Test with 5 users, fix the top problems, test again with 5 more. This iterative approach finds and fixes more problems than testing with 15 users once because you cannot fix what you have not found, and the first round of fixes reveals new problems that were masked by the original ones

Watching a participant struggle without helping is the hardest facilitator skill. If the participant asks 'Should I click this?', respond with 'What would you do if I were not here?' Helping the participant gives them the answer but gives you no data. The struggle IS the data

The System Usability Scale produces a score from 0 to 100. Below 50 is unacceptable. 50-70 is marginal. Above 70 is acceptable. Above 80 is good. Above 90 is excellent. The average SUS score across products is 68. SUS is useful for benchmarking and tracking improvement over time, not for diagnosing specific problems

Short video clips of participants struggling are the most persuasive evidence for stakeholders. A 30-second clip of a real user unable to find the checkout button is more convincing than a 10-page report. Include 2-3 video clips per finding in your report to make the problems visceral and undeniable

learning_objectives: Apply WCAG 2.1 AA guidelines to make web and mobile interfaces accessible to people with disabilities
Test interfaces with screen readers, keyboard navigation, and automated accessibility tools
Design for the full range of human abilities including visual, motor, cognitive, and auditory diversity
readings: W3C Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1 Overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Accessibility is not a feature to be added late in development or a checklist to be completed for compliance. It is a fundamental quality attribute that determines whether your product can be used by the 1.3 billion people worldwide who have a disability, and it improves the experience for everyone else through the curb cut effect. Curb cuts, ramps cut into sidewalk curbs, were designed for wheelchair users but are used daily by parents with strollers, travelers with suitcases, workers with carts, and cyclists. Similarly, captions designed for deaf users help everyone in noisy environments, high contrast designed for low-vision users helps everyone in bright sunlight, keyboard navigation designed for motor-impaired users helps power users who prefer keyboard shortcuts, and simple language designed for cognitive disabilities helps non-native speakers and everyone in a hurry. The Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) provide a comprehensive framework organized around four principles: Perceivable (users can perceive the content), Operable (users can interact with the interface), Understandable (users can comprehend the content and interface), and Robust (content works across assistive technologies). This session teaches you to design and build accessible interfaces from the ground up, not as an afterthought.
key_concepts: WCAG four principles: Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust (POUR)
WCAG conformance levels: A (minimum), AA (standard target for most organizations), AAA (highest, rarely required in full)
Color contrast requirements: 4.5:1 minimum for normal text, 3:1 for large text (18pt+ or 14pt+ bold) at AA level
Keyboard accessibility: all functionality operable via keyboard alone, visible focus indicators, logical tab order
Screen reader compatibility: semantic HTML (headings, landmarks, lists), ARIA labels for custom components, alt text for images
Alternative text: descriptive alt for informative images, empty alt for decorative images, complex image descriptions via longdesc or aria-describedby
Focus management: moving focus to dialogs when they open, returning focus when they close, trapping focus within modals
Motion and animation: respecting prefers-reduced-motion, providing pause/stop controls for auto-playing content
Forms accessibility: associated labels, error identification, input purpose (autocomplete attributes), clear instructions
Inclusive design philosophy: designing for the full range of human diversity rather than the average or typical user
lecture_notes.demonstration: Audit a web application for accessibility: run axe DevTools to identify automated violations, test keyboard navigation through the main user flow (noting missing focus indicators and tab order issues), use VoiceOver screen reader to experience the application as a blind user would (noting missing headings, unlabeled buttons, and inaccessible custom components), fix the top 5 critical issues (add heading structure, label form fields, fix contrast, add keyboard support to custom dropdown, manage modal focus), and verify fixes with retesting
setup_requirements: Web application for auditing (can use a class project or public website)
axe DevTools browser extension
VoiceOver (macOS) or NVDA (Windows) screen reader
Color contrast analyzer tool (WebAIM Contrast Checker)
Keyboard for keyboard-only testing

review.value: Every accessibility improvement helps more people than its target audience. Captions: deaf users, non-native speakers, noisy environments, quiet environments. High contrast: low vision, bright sunlight. Keyboard navigation: motor impairment, power users, broken trackpad. Simple language: cognitive disability, non-native speakers, stressed or distracted users

Semantic elements (button, nav, main, article, heading levels) provide built-in accessibility: screen readers announce them correctly, keyboards navigate them naturally, and browsers style them consistently. ARIA attributes should supplement, not replace, semantic HTML. A div with role='button' is always inferior to a real button element

The first rule of ARIA is: do not use ARIA if you can use a native HTML element. The second rule: do not change native semantics unnecessarily. ARIA adds complexity and can introduce accessibility bugs if used incorrectly. Incorrect ARIA (wrong roles, missing states) is worse than no ARIA because it provides misleading information to assistive technology

Passing automated accessibility tests catches only 30-40% of real accessibility barriers. The remaining 60-70% require manual testing: keyboard navigation, screen reader usage, cognitive evaluation, and testing with actual disabled users. Compliance with WCAG rules is necessary but not sufficient for genuine accessibility

learning_objectives: Design for mobile-specific constraints: small screens, touch input, variable contexts, and interruptions
Apply mobile design patterns for thumb-friendly interaction, gesture navigation, and responsive layouts
Evaluate mobile usability with remote unmoderated testing and analytics
readings: Apple Human Interface Guidelines: iOS Design Themes
Google Material Design: Mobile Design Guidelines

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile HCI addresses the unique challenges of designing for devices that people carry with them constantly, use in brief intermittent sessions, interact with primarily via touch, and operate in diverse and often distracting environments. A desktop user sits at a desk with full attention, a large screen, and precise mouse input. A mobile user might be walking, commuting, cooking, or lying in bed, with one hand available, variable lighting and noise, and interruptions from notifications, phone calls, and the physical world. These constraints demand design approaches that differ fundamentally from desktop: content must be prioritized ruthlessly because screen real estate is scarce, touch targets must be large enough for imprecise finger taps, interactions must be completable in brief sessions because users will be interrupted, and the interface must adapt to the way users hold their device (one-handed thumb reach, two-handed, on a table). This session covers mobile-specific design principles, platform conventions that differ between iOS and Android, and the emerging patterns for designing in a mobile-first world.
key_concepts: Mobile context of use: variable attention, one-handed use, environmental distractions (noise, sunlight, motion), brief sessions
Thumb zone: areas of the screen easily reachable by thumb in one-handed use (bottom-center is easiest, top corners are hardest)
Touch target sizes: minimum 44x44pt (Apple) or 48x48dp (Google) with adequate spacing to prevent accidental taps
Mobile navigation patterns: tab bar (iOS), bottom navigation (Android), navigation drawer for secondary items
Content prioritization: progressive disclosure showing essential content first, details on demand
Gesture navigation: swipe back, pull to refresh, swipe to delete, pinch to zoom with discoverability challenges
Mobile typography: minimum 16px body text, adequate line spacing, high contrast for outdoor readability
Adaptive vs responsive design: responsive adjusts layout to screen size, adaptive serves different designs for different device classes
Mobile form design: large input fields, appropriate keyboard types (email, number, phone), inline validation, smart defaults
Platform conventions: iOS back behavior (swipe from left edge) vs Android (hardware/gesture back button), differing navigation patterns and visual styles
lecture_notes.demonstration: Redesign a desktop web form for mobile use: identify the problems (tiny form fields, mouse-dependent interactions, too much content visible at once), apply mobile constraints (thumb zone placement for submit button, appropriate touch targets, proper keyboard types, progressive disclosure hiding optional fields), create a Figma prototype, and test the prototype on a physical device to verify touch target sizes and thumb reachability
setup_requirements: Figma with mobile device frames (iPhone and Android)
Physical mobile device for prototype testing
Thumb zone overlay template for Figma
Desktop form to redesign as the exercise starting point

review.value: Place primary navigation and actions at the bottom of the screen where thumbs can easily reach them. The old pattern of placing everything at the top (inherited from desktop) is a mobile anti-pattern because the top of the screen is the hardest area to reach one-handed, especially on modern tall phones

Gestures are powerful but invisible. There is no visual affordance for 'swipe left to delete'. Solutions: provide visible alternative triggers (edit button reveals delete), use onboarding hints on first use, ensure gestures supplement (not replace) visible controls. A gesture-only interface excludes users with motor impairments

review.key: Mobile Performance Is a UX Issue
review.value: Mobile users are more sensitive to performance than desktop users because they expect instant responsiveness from a device they carry. A 100ms delay on desktop is imperceptible; on mobile it feels sluggish. Every additional second of load time increases bounce rate by 10-20%. Performance optimization is not just engineering; it is UX

Mobile usage is characterized by frequent interruptions: phone calls, notifications, putting the phone in a pocket, arriving at a destination. Design so that users can leave and return without losing their place: auto-save form progress, maintain scroll position, preserve state across app restarts, and make re-entry easy

learning_objectives: Design conversational interfaces for voice assistants and chatbots using dialogue design principles
Handle the unique UX challenges of voice: discoverability, error recovery, and no visual feedback
Evaluate when conversational UI is appropriate versus when traditional GUI is superior

lecture_notes.introduction: Voice and conversational interfaces represent a fundamental departure from the visual, point-and-click paradigm that has dominated HCI since the Macintosh. Instead of seeing options and selecting them, users speak or type natural language requests and the system interprets and responds. Smart speakers (Alexa, Google Home), voice assistants on phones (Siri, Google Assistant), and text-based chatbots in customer service have made conversational UI mainstream, with over 4 billion devices worldwide supporting voice interaction. However, conversational UI introduces unique design challenges that visual interfaces solved decades ago. Discoverability is the most significant: in a GUI, users can see all available options; in a voice interface, there is no menu to browse, no buttons to read, no visual feedback to guide exploration. Users must somehow know what they can say, and the system must handle the infinite variety of natural language gracefully, including misunderstandings, ambiguous requests, and out-of-scope commands. This session teaches the principles of conversation design: how to structure dialogues, handle errors naturally, provide appropriate feedback through audio and visual channels, and determine when voice is genuinely better than a screen.
key_concepts: Conversational design principles: cooperative (helpful), goal-oriented, contextual (remembers state), and turns (back and forth exchange)
Dialogue flow design: intents (what the user wants), utterances (how they say it), entities (key parameters), and responses
Prompt design: open prompts ('How can I help?'), directed prompts ('What city?'), and re-prompts for clarification
Error handling in voice: no-input (user said nothing), no-match (system did not understand), and out-of-scope (not supported)
Confirmation strategies: implicit ('Booking a table for 2 at 7pm'), explicit ('You want 2 people at 7pm, is that right?'), and mixed
Voice UI personality: consistent tone, appropriate formality, brand alignment, and the uncanny valley of over-human behavior
Multimodal design: combining voice with visual displays (smart displays, phone screens) for the best of both modalities
Chatbot design patterns: menu-driven (buttons), free-text, hybrid (suggested replies with free-text option), and handoff to human agent
Natural Language Understanding limitations: ambiguity, context dependence, accents, background noise, and the long tail of rare utterances
Discoverability solutions: sample utterances, progressive disclosure of capabilities, help commands, and visual companion apps
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design a voice interface for a recipe assistant: map the dialogue flow from 'I want to cook something' through recipe selection, ingredient review, and step-by-step cooking instructions, handle error scenarios (user says ingredient not recognized, user asks to go back, timer runs out), design confirmation strategies that do not feel tedious, create a multimodal version that shows recipe steps on a screen while reading them aloud, and compare the voice-first experience against a traditional app for the same task
setup_requirements: Dialogue flow diagramming tool
Voice prototyping tool (Voiceflow, Dialogflow, or Amazon Alexa Developer Console)
Recording equipment for voice prototype testing
Smart display mockup templates for multimodal design

review.key: When Voice Wins vs When GUI Wins
review.value: Voice excels for: hands-busy/eyes-busy contexts (cooking, driving), quick queries with simple answers (weather, timer), accessibility for visually impaired users. GUI excels for: browsing/comparing options, complex inputs (forms, documents), private contexts (public spaces), and tasks requiring visual reference. Use multimodal design when possible

Users cannot discover voice features by looking at a screen. Most voice assistant users know only 5-10 commands despite the system supporting hundreds. Address with: onboarding that teaches key commands, suggested follow-up actions after each interaction, a companion app or website listing capabilities, and 'What can you do?' help command

In conversation, humans naturally ask for clarification. Voice UIs must do the same without feeling robotic. Instead of 'Sorry, I did not understand', try 'I heard [X], did you mean [Y] or [Z]?' Offer specific alternatives rather than generic error messages. Escalate to a human agent after two failed attempts

Define the assistant's personality (helpful but not chatty, professional but not cold) and apply it consistently. Write scripts that sound natural when spoken aloud, not like written text read verbatim. Avoid humor that may not translate across cultures. Never pretend the system is human when it is not

learning_objectives: Understand the unique interaction design challenges of immersive 3D environments
Apply spatial UI design principles for head-mounted displays and AR overlays
Evaluate VR/AR usability including motion sickness, spatial navigation, and input modalities
readings: Apple visionOS Human Interface Guidelines: Spatial Design

lecture_notes.introduction: Virtual Reality and Augmented Reality represent the newest frontier of human-computer interaction, moving interface design from flat screens into three-dimensional space. VR replaces the user's visual environment entirely with a synthetic world. AR overlays digital content onto the physical world. Both introduce interaction design challenges that have no precedent in 2D interface design: how do you place a menu in 3D space? How do you indicate a clickable object when there is no cursor? How do you prevent motion sickness caused by visual-vestibular conflict? How do you design for a field of view that moves with the user's head? The Apple Vision Pro and Meta Quest have brought spatial computing to consumer markets, making VR/AR design skills increasingly relevant. This session covers the fundamental principles of designing for immersive environments: spatial layout, gaze and hand tracking input, comfort and ergonomics, and the unique usability challenges of environments where users physically move through the interface. We ground this in existing HCI principles (Gestalt, affordances, feedback) while introducing the new principles required by the third dimension.
key_concepts: Spatial UI placement: content zones relative to user position (near field for interaction, far field for ambient information)
Depth and scale: using size consistency, aerial perspective, and stereoscopic depth cues for spatial hierarchy
Gaze-based interaction: eye tracking for selection, dwell time for activation, challenges of Midas touch (everything you look at activates)
Hand tracking and gesture input: pinch to select, grab and move, palm-up for menu, gesture vocabulary limitations
Controller-based input: ray casting (pointing a virtual laser), direct manipulation (touching virtual objects), and hybrid approaches
Motion sickness mitigation: maintaining high frame rate (90fps+), reducing vection (visual motion without physical motion), providing stable reference points
Comfort zones: content within 1-3 meters feels natural; too close causes eye strain, too far reduces readability
AR world-anchoring: attaching digital content to physical objects with SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)
Field of view considerations: placing critical content within the comfortable viewing cone (approximately 30 degrees from center)
Accessibility in XR: seated mode alternatives, voice input for motor impairment, audio descriptions for low vision
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design an AR interior design application: place virtual furniture in a real room using AR surface detection, implement gaze-based selection (look at furniture to highlight it), hand gesture for grabbing and repositioning items, compare the spatial UI of object properties panel (floating near the selected object vs fixed position in space), evaluate comfort by testing different content distances and interaction methods, and identify accessibility barriers for users who cannot perform hand gestures
setup_requirements: AR/VR prototyping tool (ShapesXR, Figma with spatial design plugins, or Unity)
VR headset or AR-capable device for demonstration
360-degree video recording for VR walkthroughs
Spatial audio setup for immersive prototypes

review.value: VR must maintain 90 frames per second minimum to avoid motion sickness. At lower frame rates, the visual system detects a discrepancy between head movement and visual update, triggering nausea. This is a hard constraint: unlike web or mobile where a slow frame rate is merely annoying, in VR it is physically sickening

review.key: Spatial UI is Not Desktop UI in 3D
review.value: Floating 2D windows in VR is the laziest spatial design. True spatial UI leverages the medium: content attached to physical surfaces in AR, information arranged in a radial layout around the user in VR, depth for information hierarchy, and spatial audio for notification direction. Design for the affordances of 3D space

review.key: AR Content Must Respect Physical Reality
review.value: AR content that ignores physics feels broken: virtual objects floating in mid-air, intersecting with walls, or appearing at impossible scales destroy the illusion. Use plane detection, occlusion (real objects hiding virtual ones), and lighting estimation to make digital content feel like it belongs in the physical space

No single input works for all VR/AR tasks. Gaze: good for selection, bad for precise positioning. Hand tracking: natural but fatiguing over time (gorilla arm syndrome). Controllers: precise but require holding a physical object. Voice: hands-free but not private. Design for the primary input and provide fallbacks for the others

learning_objectives: Create a design system with reusable components, design tokens, and usage documentation
Implement design system governance including contribution, versioning, and deprecation processes
Bridge the design-development handoff with Figma-to-code alignment and shared vocabulary
readings: Figma Documentation: Design Systems and Libraries

lecture_notes.introduction: A design system is the single source of truth for how a digital product looks, feels, and behaves, consisting of reusable components, design tokens (colors, typography, spacing), patterns, and documentation that enable teams to build consistent, high-quality interfaces at scale. Without a design system, large products inevitably drift: different teams implement buttons differently, spacing is inconsistent across screens, color usage is arbitrary, and the product feels like a collection of unrelated features rather than a coherent experience. Design systems solve this by codifying design decisions into shared libraries that designers and developers both reference. Material Design (Google), Human Interface Guidelines (Apple), Fluent Design (Microsoft), and Carbon (IBM) are public design systems that demonstrate the approach at scale. This session teaches you to create, document, and govern a design system that bridges the gap between design and development, ensuring that the Figma components designers use match the coded components developers build, and that both teams share a common vocabulary for discussing interface decisions.
key_concepts: Design tokens: named values for colors, typography, spacing, shadows, and other visual properties that can be shared between Figma and code
Component library: reusable UI components (buttons, inputs, cards, modals) with defined variants, states, and usage guidelines
Atomic design: organizing components hierarchically (atoms: button, label; molecules: form field; organisms: form; templates: page layout)
Component documentation: description, usage guidelines, do/don't examples, accessibility requirements, and code API
Design-development alignment: matching Figma component properties to code component props for seamless handoff
Versioning and changelog: semantic versioning for the design system, documenting breaking changes, and migration guides
Contribution model: how teams propose new components, the review process, and acceptance criteria
Design tokens in code: CSS custom properties, theme files, or style dictionaries that consume the same token values as Figma
Governance: who owns the design system, how decisions are made, how consistency is enforced, and how exceptions are handled
Design system adoption: measuring usage, identifying drift, and creating incentives for adoption over ad-hoc solutions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a mini design system in Figma: define design tokens (color palette with semantic names, type scale, spacing scale), create a button component with variants (primary/secondary/outline, small/medium/large, enabled/disabled/loading), document usage guidelines with do/don't examples, create a card component that uses the button, show how changing a token (primary color) propagates throughout all components, and demonstrate how the Figma component structure maps to a React component API
setup_requirements: Figma with team library enabled
Design token definition template
Component documentation template
Reference design system (Material Design) for comparison

review.key: Design Tokens as Single Source of Truth
review.value: When a color is defined as a token (color-primary: #1976D2) and used everywhere by reference, changing it once updates the entire product. Without tokens, the same blue appears as 15 slightly different hex values across the codebase. Tokens enable theme switching (dark mode) and brand customization with zero component changes

A button component without documentation will be used incorrectly: primary buttons everywhere (defeating their purpose), wrong sizes for contexts, disabled buttons without explanation. Documentation specifies when to use each variant, maximum label length, icon placement rules, and accessibility requirements

Building a design system is expensive upfront (months of effort). The ROI comes from consistency (fewer design reviews), speed (assembling from components rather than designing from scratch), quality (pre-tested, accessible components), and onboarding (new team members learn the system, not tribal knowledge). ROI is typically positive within 6-12 months for teams of 5+ designers and developers

Without governance, a design system becomes a neglected artifact. With too much governance, it becomes a bottleneck. Balance by: having a dedicated maintainer (even part-time), setting a regular release cadence, creating a lightweight RFC process for new components, and empowering teams to extend (not fork) the system for specialized needs

learning_objectives: Present a complete user-centered design project demonstrating research, design, testing, and iteration
Critically evaluate peer projects for research rigor, design quality, and evidence-based decision making
Synthesize HCI principles into a personal design practice framework
readings: Norman, The Design of Everyday Things (review key chapters)

lecture_notes.introduction: The final week brings together everything you have learned in this course through project presentations and a comprehensive review of HCI principles and methods. Each student presents their complete user-centered design project, demonstrating the full UCD cycle: user research that revealed genuine needs, task analysis that structured the problem, information architecture that organized the solution, wireframes and prototypes that explored design alternatives, visual design that communicated hierarchy and brand, usability testing that validated (or invalidated) design assumptions, and iterative refinement that improved the design based on evidence. Presentations are evaluated not on whether the final design is perfect (no design is) but on whether every design decision is grounded in research evidence, whether usability problems were discovered and addressed, and whether the designer can articulate the reasoning behind their choices. After presentations, we review the HCI toolkit and discuss the evolving landscape: AI-generated interfaces, spatial computing, brain-computer interfaces, and the growing recognition that design ethics must be central to HCI practice.
key_concepts: Design process reflection: what worked, what did not, what would you do differently with more time or resources
Evidence-based design: every decision justified by research data, not personal preference or stakeholder opinion
Design evolution story: showing how the design changed from initial concept through testing to final version, with the reasons for each change
Presentation structure: problem statement, research summary, design process, key design decisions with rationale, usability test results, iterations, final design, future recommendations
Portfolio documentation: organizing the project as a case study that demonstrates UCD skills for future employment
HCI career paths: UX researcher, UX designer, interaction designer, information architect, accessibility specialist, design system lead
Emerging HCI challenges: AI-generated interfaces, ethical implications of persuasive design, designing for AI-human collaboration
The HCI practitioner's ethical responsibilities: designing for all users, respecting user autonomy, avoiding dark patterns, and considering societal impact
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through an exemplary final project: a redesign of a public library catalog system, from initial research (interviewing 8 patrons revealing that search was fine but browsing was broken), through card sorting (discovering users think by mood and topic, not by Dewey Decimal), to an interactive prototype with a mood-based browsing feature, usability testing (4 of 5 users completed tasks faster), and final iteration, highlighting the evidence chain from research finding to design decision to validated outcome
setup_requirements: Presentation slides or interactive portfolio
Figma prototype for live demonstration
Usability test video clips for evidence
Peer evaluation rubric forms

review.key: The UCD Process Matters More Than the Output
review.value: A beautiful design created without user research may solve the wrong problem. An ugly prototype tested with real users that reveals three critical usability issues is more valuable. The process of understanding users, designing for their needs, testing your assumptions, and iterating based on evidence is the core skill. Visual polish can always be added later

Every shipped design is a hypothesis about what works for users. Usage data, support tickets, and ongoing research reveal whether the hypothesis was correct. The best designs evolve continuously based on evidence. Treat launch as the beginning of the next research cycle, not the end of the design process

review.key: Ethical Design Is a Professional Obligation
review.value: HCI practitioners have unique power to shape how people spend their time, make decisions, and interact with information. Dark patterns (deceptive design that tricks users into unintended actions) are a professional ethics violation. Design for user benefit, not just business metrics. When business goals conflict with user wellbeing, advocate for the user

review.key: HCI Is a Lifelong Learning Practice
review.value: New platforms (spatial computing, AI assistants), new users (aging population, global south), and new challenges (privacy, attention economy, misinformation) continuously expand the scope of HCI. Stay current through academic research (CHI Conference), industry practice (Nielsen Norman Group), and direct contact with users. The practitioner who stops learning designs for yesterday's users

quizzes.topic: HCI Foundations, User Research, and Task Analysis

questions.question: A user clicks a 'Save' button and nothing visible happens for 3 seconds while the server processes the request. Identify which Nielsen heuristic is violated, explain why it is problematic from a cognitive perspective, and describe three design solutions of increasing quality.
questions.answer: Violated heuristic: Visibility of system status. The system should keep users informed about what is happening through appropriate feedback within reasonable time. Cognitive basis: Without feedback, the user's mental model cannot be updated. They do not know if the click registered, if the save is in progress, or if it failed. This creates uncertainty, which triggers anxiety and repeat clicking (potentially causing duplicate submissions). Three solutions (increasing quality): (1) Basic: Show a loading spinner immediately on click. User knows the save is in progress but not how long it will take. (2) Better: Disable the Save button and change text to 'Saving...' with a spinner. Prevents duplicate submissions and communicates state. Show a success toast when complete. (3) Best: Optimistic save with animated confirmation. Immediately show a brief green checkmark animation on the button ('Saved!'), process the save in the background. If it fails (rare), show an error notification with retry. This provides instant feedback for the 99% success case while handling the 1% failure case gracefully.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies visibility of system status heuristic
Explains cognitive basis (uncertainty, anxiety, duplicate actions)
Three solutions showing progressive improvement with rationale

questions.question: You are designing a nurse scheduling application. Describe how you would conduct a contextual inquiry in a hospital ward, including your preparation, observation protocol, and how you would handle the ethical challenges of observing in a healthcare environment.
questions.answer: Preparation: (1) Obtain IRB/ethics board approval for observing in a healthcare setting. (2) Coordinate with ward management for appropriate observation times. (3) Prepare a master-apprentice protocol: the nurse is the expert, you are the apprentice learning their work. (4) Develop an observation guide focusing on scheduling tasks, communication patterns, and workarounds. Observation protocol: (1) Shadow 3-4 nurses during different shifts (day, night, weekend) for 2-3 hours each. (2) Observe their scheduling workflow in context: how they check the schedule, request changes, handle call-outs, and communicate with charge nurses. (3) Ask questions during natural pauses (not during patient care): 'I noticed you wrote that on a sticky note; can you tell me about that?' (4) Note the physical environment: shared computer, interruption frequency, paper vs digital tools, communication channels. (5) Document workarounds: unofficial practices that the current system does not support. Ethical handling: (1) Never observe patient care directly or record patient information. (2) Step away during sensitive conversations. (3) Informed consent from all observed nurses with option to decline or stop at any time. (4) Clear boundaries: you are observing the scheduling process, not evaluating nurse performance. (5) Anonymize all data in notes and reports. (6) Share findings with participants before publication to verify accuracy.

rubric.key: Describes preparation including ethics approval and observation guide
Protocol covers multiple shifts, in-context questioning, and workaround documentation
Addresses ethical challenges specific to healthcare observation
Mentions informed consent and data anonymization

questions.question: Explain Fitts's Law and apply it to redesign a mobile app toolbar. The current toolbar has five small (24x24px) icons evenly spaced at the top of the screen. Describe what you would change and why.
questions.answer: Fitts's Law states that the time to reach a target is proportional to the distance divided by the target size: MT = a + b * log2(D/W + 1). Larger targets and closer targets are faster to reach. Redesign changes: (1) Move the toolbar to the bottom of the screen. On mobile, the bottom is within thumb reach (0-50mm distance); the top requires repositioning the hand (100-150mm distance). According to Fitts's Law, the closer position significantly reduces movement time. (2) Increase icon touch targets to at least 48x48dp (Google recommendation). Larger targets reduce movement time according to the W (width) component and reduce error rate (tapping the wrong icon). The visual icon can remain smaller (24px) with the tap target extending beyond the visible area. (3) Add spacing between icons to prevent accidental adjacent taps. This effectively increases the functional target size by eliminating the ambiguous zone between targets. (4) Place the most-used icon in the center-bottom position (easiest thumb reach). Order the remaining icons by frequency of use, with least-used at the edges. (5) Consider grouping related icons to leverage Fitts's Law for sequential actions: if users often tap icon A then icon B, placing them adjacent reduces total movement time.

rubric.key: Correctly states Fitts's Law with distance and size components
Moves toolbar to bottom with thumb reach justification
Increases touch targets with specific size recommendations
Addresses icon ordering by frequency and spacing for error prevention

questions.question: You conducted an open card sort with 15 participants for an intranet redesign. Three distinct organizational patterns emerged, each favored by about 5 participants. How do you interpret this result and what do you do next?
questions.answer: Interpretation: Three equally popular patterns mean there is no single correct information architecture. This typically indicates one of two situations: (1) Different user roles organize information differently (e.g., engineers by technical topic, managers by project, HR by process). (2) Multiple valid mental models exist and none dominates. Next steps: (1) Analyze whether the patterns correlate with participant characteristics (role, department, tenure). If so, the users are different audience segments that may need different navigation paths. (2) Look for areas of agreement across all three patterns: items that everyone grouped together belong together regardless of the top-level structure. (3) Consider a polyhierarchical approach: place content in multiple categories so users with different mental models can all find it through their preferred path. (4) Run a closed card sort where participants sort items into each of the three candidate structures to quantify which structure has the highest overall success rate. (5) Supplement with a tree test: implement the structure that best accommodates all three patterns and test whether users can find items. (6) Consider hybrid navigation: task-based primary navigation (accommodating the most common pattern) with role-based or topic-based secondary navigation.

rubric.key: Correctly interprets multiple patterns as diverse mental models
Investigates correlation with user characteristics
Proposes polyhierarchy or hybrid navigation solution
Plans validation with closed sort or tree test
