questions.question: Design a usability test plan for a flight booking website. Include: target participants (with screening criteria), three specific task scenarios, quantitative and qualitative metrics to collect, and the exact think-aloud instructions you would give participants.
questions.answer: Participants: 5 adults, age 25-55, who have booked at least 2 flights online in the past year. Screen out: travel industry professionals, UX professionals, and anyone who has used this specific website. Mix: 2 frequent travelers (monthly), 3 occasional travelers (2-4 times/year). Recruitment via usertesting.com or local recruiting agency. Tasks: (1) 'Your friend invited you to visit them in Miami. Find and book the cheapest round-trip flight from Chicago to Miami, departing March 15 and returning March 22, for one adult.' (2) 'You just booked a flight but realized you need to add a checked bag. Add one checked bag to your booking.' (3) 'Plans changed. Change the departure date of your booked flight from March 15 to March 17.' Metrics: Quantitative: Task completion rate (binary: completed/not), Time on task (seconds), Number of errors (wrong clicks, backtracking), Post-task difficulty rating (1-5 Likert scale per task), System Usability Scale (SUS) post-session survey. Qualitative: Think-aloud observations (confusion points, expectations, workarounds), post-task follow-up questions, and post-session interview about overall experience. Think-aloud instructions: 'As you work through each task, I would like you to think out loud. Tell me what you are looking at, what you are thinking about, and what you expect to happen when you click or tap something. If you are confused or unsure, say so. There are no wrong answers. We are testing the website, not you. I cannot help you during the tasks, but please ask anything you want during breaks between tasks.'

rubric.key: Specific participant criteria with screening and mix
Three realistic, measurable task scenarios with context
Both quantitative metrics (completion, time, errors, SUS) and qualitative methods
Proper think-aloud instructions with no-help and no-judgment framing

questions.question: Explain why using a high-fidelity prototype too early in the design process is counterproductive. What specific feedback problems does it create?
questions.answer: High-fidelity prototypes too early create three specific problems: (1) Aesthetic bias: Users and stakeholders focus on visual details (color choice, font, image selection) instead of structural issues (navigation, information hierarchy, task flow). A wireframe forces feedback on structure because there is nothing visual to comment on. (2) Sunk cost attachment: A high-fidelity prototype takes days to create. The team becomes invested in the specific solution and resists fundamental changes that testing might suggest. A paper sketch takes minutes, making it psychologically easy to discard and redesign. (3) False confidence: A polished-looking prototype feels finished, creating pressure to ship rather than iterate. Stakeholders see a hi-fi mockup and ask 'When can we build this?' instead of 'Let us test this with users.' The prototype's polish signals completion when the design has not been validated. (4) Scope anchoring: The specific layout, component choices, and interactions in a hi-fi prototype narrow the design space. The team iterates within the frame of the prototype instead of exploring fundamentally different approaches that might solve the problem better. Start with low-fidelity to explore the problem space broadly, then increase fidelity as the design converges.

rubric.key: Identifies aesthetic bias diverting feedback from structure
Explains sunk cost attachment reducing willingness to change
Mentions false confidence or scope anchoring

questions.question: A web form has red error messages that appear next to fields with invalid input. Explain three specific WCAG accessibility guidelines this design may violate and provide a fully accessible alternative design.
questions.answer: WCAG violations: (1) 1.4.1 Use of Color: Using only red color to indicate errors fails this guideline because users with color vision deficiency (8% of men) may not perceive the red. Fix: Add an error icon (exclamation mark) and descriptive text alongside the color change. Do not rely on color alone. (2) 4.1.3 Status Messages: If error messages appear dynamically without ARIA live regions, screen readers will not announce them. The user submits, errors appear visually, but the screen reader user does not know errors exist. Fix: Wrap error messages in an element with role='alert' or aria-live='assertive' so screen readers announce them automatically. (3) 1.3.1 Info and Relationships: If error messages are not programmatically associated with their form fields, screen reader users cannot tell which field has which error. Fix: Use aria-describedby on the input field referencing the error message ID. When the user tabs to the field, the screen reader reads both the label and the error. Fully accessible design: Error message container with role='alert'. Error text preceded by an icon with aria-hidden='true' (decorative since the text is sufficient). Error message has a unique ID. Input has aria-describedby pointing to the error ID. Input has aria-invalid='true'. Error text meets 4.5:1 contrast ratio. Error text includes the field name ('Email address is required') for context when announced.

rubric.key: Identifies three specific WCAG guidelines with numbers
Explains the real-world impact on disabled users for each violation
Provides complete accessible alternative with ARIA attributes

questions.question: Explain the Gestalt principle of proximity and apply it to solve this design problem: a settings screen has 20 toggle switches with labels, and users report they cannot find the setting they need. The current layout places all 20 toggles in a single flat list.
questions.answer: Gestalt principle of proximity: elements near each other are perceived as belonging to the same group. The brain automatically creates visual groups based on spatial relationships before conscious analysis. This is the strongest and most reliable grouping cue. Application to the settings screen: Group the 20 toggles into 4-5 logical categories (e.g., Notifications, Privacy, Appearance, Account, Advanced) using proximity: (1) Add visible section headings in a larger/bolder font that act as scannable landmarks. (2) Place related toggles close together (8px apart) within each group. (3) Separate groups with more space (24-32px) between them. (4) The spacing difference alone creates clear visual groups without needing divider lines or background colors. (5) Users can now scan the 4-5 group headings (manageable) instead of scanning 20 individual toggles (overwhelming). Optional enhancements: (1) Make section headings collapsible so users can hide irrelevant groups. (2) Add a search field at the top for users who know the setting name. (3) Move the most-changed settings to the top. The key insight: the original flat list fails because 20 items exceeds working memory capacity (7 plus or minus 2). Grouping into 4-5 categories matches cognitive capacity perfectly.

rubric.key: Correctly defines proximity principle with visual grouping mechanism
Groups toggles into logical categories with section headings
Specifies spacing differences between within-group and between-group
Connects to cognitive capacity limits (working memory)

questions.question: When should a designer use a skeleton loading screen versus a spinner, and when should they use an optimistic UI update instead of either? Give a specific example for each.
questions.answer: Skeleton screen: Use when loading a page or section where the layout is predictable. Example: loading a social media feed. Show gray placeholder rectangles for each post (profile picture circle, name bar, content block). This communicates what is coming, creates a sense of progress, and is perceived as 10-20% faster than a spinner for the same actual load time. Best for: content-heavy pages with known layouts loading in 1-5 seconds. Spinner: Use when the layout is unpredictable or the operation is not a content load. Example: processing a payment. The user clicked 'Pay' and the result could be success, failure, or a redirect. A skeleton screen does not make sense because you do not know what will appear. A spinner with contextual text ('Processing your payment...') is appropriate. Best for: indeterminate operations under 10 seconds where the outcome determines what appears next. Optimistic UI: Use when the action succeeds 99%+ of the time and the UI change is immediately obvious. Example: tapping the 'Like' button on a social media post. Immediately fill the heart icon and increment the count. Send the API request in the background. If it fails (rare), revert the heart and show a brief error. The user perceives instant response. Best for: simple, reversible actions with very high success rates where any loading state would feel sluggish.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies skeleton for predictable content layouts
Correctly identifies spinner for indeterminate or unpredictable outcomes
Correctly identifies optimistic UI for high-success reversible actions
Provides specific appropriate examples for each

quizzes.topic: Usability Testing, Accessibility, and Mobile HCI

questions.question: During a usability test, a participant gets visibly frustrated and says 'This is so confusing, I would never use this app.' How do you respond as the facilitator? What data do you record, and how do you handle the remainder of the session?
questions.answer: Immediate response: Acknowledge the frustration without apologizing for the design or trying to help: 'I can see that is frustrating. That is exactly the kind of feedback we need. Can you tell me what you expected to happen?' This validates the participant's experience, reminds them their feedback is valuable, and redirects to productive observation. Do NOT: explain how the feature works, apologize, or offer to skip the task. Data to record: (1) The exact point where frustration occurred (screen, element, action). (2) What the participant was trying to do and what they expected. (3) What actually happened (the gap between expectation and reality). (4) Verbatim quote for the report. (5) Body language and emotional state. (6) Whether they eventually completed the task or abandoned it. Remainder of session: (1) If the participant wants to continue: move to the next task. Remind them 'We have just a couple more things to try.' (2) If the participant is too frustrated: offer to take a short break. If they want to stop, respect that decision. Thank them for their contribution and end the session. Never pressure a participant to continue. (3) Record the uncompleted task as a task failure in results. (4) Note this as a potential catastrophic usability issue in findings. If multiple participants show similar frustration at the same point, it is a critical redesign priority.

rubric.key: Appropriate facilitator response: validate without helping or apologizing
Specific data points to record including verbatim quote and failure point
Handles session continuation sensitively with respect for participant autonomy

questions.question: Explain the thumb zone concept in mobile design and why it matters more for modern smartphones than for older devices. How does it affect the placement of primary navigation and action buttons?
questions.answer: The thumb zone is the area of the screen that a user can comfortably reach with their thumb while holding the phone in one hand. Steven Hoober's research mapped three zones: Easy (green, bottom-center), OK (yellow, sides and lower portions), Hard (red, top corners and far edges). Why it matters more now: Modern smartphones have screens 6-7 inches tall compared to 3.5-4 inches a decade ago. On a 6.5-inch phone, the top 40% of the screen is physically unreachable without shifting grip. The old pattern of placing navigation bars and action buttons at the top of the screen (inherited from desktop browser conventions) forces users to constantly reposition their hand or use two hands for what should be one-handed operations. Design implications: (1) Primary navigation: Place at the bottom of the screen (iOS tab bar, Android bottom navigation). Material Design moved from top navigation drawer to bottom navigation for this reason. (2) Primary actions: Place the main call-to-action button in the bottom-right quadrant (easiest thumb reach for right-handed majority) or bottom-center. (3) Destructive actions: Place in the hard-to-reach zone (top corners) so accidental taps are less likely. (4) Content: Place scannable content in the center and upper area (where eyes look) but interactive elements in the lower area (where thumbs reach). (5) Consider reachability features: iOS Reachability (double-tap home to slide screen down) exists because the top is unreachable.

rubric.key: Correctly defines thumb zone with easy/ok/hard regions
Explains why larger modern screens make this more critical
Specific design recommendations for navigation and action placement

questions.question: A voice assistant user says 'Play something chill' to a music app. Design the dialogue flow for this interaction, including how you handle ambiguity, confirmation, and a follow-up request to change the song.
questions.answer:
Dialogue flow:

User: 'Play something chill'
System interpretation: Intent=play_music, mood=relaxing/chill, no specific artist/song/genre.

System response (implicit confirmation + action): 'Playing a chill mix for you. Starting with [Song Name] by [Artist].'
[Music begins playing]

Rationale: Do not ask for clarification when you have enough to act. The user said 'chill' which maps to a mood-based playlist. Start playing immediately with implicit confirmation (telling them what you chose). Asking 'What genre of chill?' or 'Did you mean jazz or ambient?' adds unnecessary friction.

Follow-up - User: 'Skip this' or 'Next'
System: 'Playing [Next Song] by [Artist].'
[Next song plays]

Follow-up - User: 'I don't like this, play something more upbeat'
System: 'Switching to an upbeat mix. Here is [Song] by [Artist].'
[New playlist begins]

Rationale: The system adjusts based on feedback without requiring a complete restart. It maintains context (user wants music) and modifies the parameter (mood: chill -> upbeat).

Error case - System cannot determine mood:
User: 'Play something blarngy'
System: 'I'm not sure what kind of music that is. Would you like to try a chill mix, an upbeat mix, or a focus playlist?'

Rationale: Offer 3 specific alternatives rather than an open 'What did you mean?' which puts the burden back on the user. Keep the conversation moving forward.

rubric.key: Plays immediately with implicit confirmation rather than asking for clarification
Handles follow-up requests maintaining conversational context
Error case offers specific alternatives instead of generic error
Includes rationale for each design decision

quizzes.topic: VR/AR, Design Systems, and HCI Synthesis

questions.question: Explain why VR must maintain 90 frames per second while mobile apps target 60fps and web applications often function at 30fps. What is the physiological mechanism that makes low VR frame rates dangerous rather than merely annoying?
questions.answer: The physiological mechanism is visual-vestibular conflict, commonly experienced as motion sickness or simulator sickness. In VR, the visual system is completely replaced by the headset display. When the user moves their head, the vestibular system (inner ear) detects the physical motion immediately and the brain expects the visual scene to update accordingly. At 60fps, there is a 16.7ms delay between frames. At 90fps, the delay is 11.1ms. Below 90fps, the delay between physical head movement and visual update becomes perceptible. The brain interprets this mismatch as a sign of poisoning (the same mechanism that causes car sickness) and triggers nausea, dizziness, and disorientation. This is a physiological response, not merely a perception of smoothness. Why other platforms tolerate lower frame rates: Mobile at 60fps: the screen does not track head movement. Visual content changes in response to touch, not physical motion, so there is no vestibular conflict. Web at 30fps: content is mostly static. Scrolling jank is annoying but does not cause nausea because the user's body is stationary and the visual motion is in a small window, not filling the entire visual field. Additional VR concerns: the display is inches from the eyes, filling the entire field of vision. Any rendering artifact (judder, ghosting, latency) is amplified compared to a screen at arm's length. This is why VR hardware dedicates massive GPU resources to maintaining frame rate.

rubric.key: Correctly explains visual-vestibular conflict as the mechanism
Explains why the brain triggers nausea (poisoning defense)
Contrasts why mobile/web tolerate lower rates (no head tracking, no full visual field replacement)

questions.question: A design system has 4 button variants: primary, secondary, outline, and text. A product team wants to add a fifth variant 'danger' for destructive actions. As the design system maintainer, describe the evaluation criteria you would use to decide whether to add it and what your recommendation would be.
questions.answer: Evaluation criteria: (1) Frequency of use: How many screens across the product need a destructive action button? If it appears on 2 screens, a one-off solution is sufficient. If it appears on 20+ screens, a system-level component is justified. (2) Consistency benefit: Are teams currently implementing destructive buttons inconsistently (different reds, different styles)? If so, a standardized variant prevents divergence. (3) Compositional alternative: Can the existing system already solve this? A primary button with a semantic color token (color: error) or an outline button in red might work without a new variant. Adding a variant when composition suffices bloats the system. (4) Accessibility impact: Does the variant maintain accessibility requirements? Red buttons need sufficient contrast, color-independent identification (icon or text like 'Delete' makes the action clear), and focus styling. (5) Adoption cost: How many teams need to update their code? Is the migration path simple? Recommendation: Add the danger variant IF destructive actions appear in 10+ locations AND teams are currently inconsistent. Implement as a semantic variant (purpose-based) not a visual variant (style-based): the 'danger' variant communicates intent to developers, not just appearance. It should include: red color scheme, an optional leading icon (trash, warning), and documentation specifying when to use it vs confirmation dialogs. If destructive actions are rare (under 5 locations), recommend a compositional approach: use the existing outline button with color='error' token rather than adding system-level complexity.

rubric.key: Lists clear evaluation criteria including frequency and consistency benefit
Considers compositional alternative before adding a new variant
Makes a justified recommendation with threshold reasoning
Addresses accessibility and documentation requirements

questions.question: Reflecting on the full HCI course, explain why a user-centered design process produces better outcomes than a stakeholder-driven process, even when the stakeholder has deep domain expertise. Provide a specific example.
questions.answer: Stakeholders with deep domain expertise suffer from the curse of knowledge: they cannot imagine not knowing what they know. This creates three specific problems: (1) They design for themselves, not for average users. An expert cardiologist designing a heart monitoring app creates an interface that makes sense to cardiologists but is incomprehensible to patients who need to use it daily. UCD discovers through research that patients think about heart health in terms of 'good days' and 'bad days', not in terms of ejection fraction percentages. (2) They confuse frequency with importance. The stakeholder uses advanced features daily and demands they be prominent. UCD reveals that 80% of users use only 3 of 30 features, and the advanced features used by the stakeholder should be in a secondary menu, not the home screen. (3) They optimize for power users and ignore onboarding. The stakeholder forgot what it was like to be a beginner. UCD's usability testing with new users reveals that the first-run experience is so confusing that 40% of users abandon the app within 5 minutes, a problem invisible to the expert stakeholder. The UCD advantage: empirical evidence from real users trumps expert opinion, no matter how experienced the expert. UCD does not ignore stakeholder expertise; it validates it against user behavior and reveals the gaps between expert assumptions and user reality.

rubric.key: Identifies curse of knowledge as the root problem
Provides a specific, concrete example demonstrating the gap
Explains how UCD's empirical evidence corrects expert assumptions

major_assignments.title: User Research and Design Specification
major_assignments.description: Conduct comprehensive user research for a real application domain: interview users, observe their current workflows, synthesize findings into personas and journey maps, perform task analysis, and deliver a design specification that translates research insights into concrete design requirements.
objectives: Conduct at least 5 user interviews with proper protocol and ethical standards
Synthesize research into personas, journey maps, and task analyses grounded in observed data
Perform card sorting to inform information architecture decisions
Produce a design specification that translates research findings into actionable design requirements
deliverables: User research report with interview guides, transcripts/notes, and affinity diagram photos
Two research-based personas with goals, behaviors, frustrations, and context narratives
User journey map with touchpoints, emotions, pain points, and design opportunities
Task analysis (HTA diagram) for the primary user task with frequency and criticality annotations
Design specification document with prioritized requirements linked to specific research findings

criteria: At least 5 user interviews conducted with proper open-ended questioning technique
Affinity diagramming reveals clear themes supported by multiple data points
Personas are based on behavioral patterns from research, not demographics or assumptions
Task analysis decomposes the primary task to actionable detail with plans and conditions
Journey map is visually clear with consistent use of elements across stages
HTA diagram follows standard notation and is readable at both overview and detail levels
Personas are presented in a format usable by a design team (not just academic documents)
Design specification is structured for reference during design and development phases
Research methods are appropriate for the questions being answered
Card sort results are analyzed quantitatively (similarity matrix) not just qualitatively
Requirements are prioritized using a clear framework (MoSCoW or frequency-criticality matrix)
Design specification is traceable: each requirement links to specific research evidence
Research report is complete with methodology, raw data, and analysis
Interview guides demonstrate proper question design (open-ended, non-leading)
Ethical considerations addressed: informed consent, anonymization, data handling
Design specification distinguishes user needs (what) from design solutions (how)
Thorough user research with proper methodology and ethical standards
Research-grounded personas and journey maps that reveal genuine user needs
Detailed task analysis identifying frequency, criticality, and error-prone steps
Actionable design specification with requirements traced to evidence

major_assignments.title: Interactive Prototype with Usability Testing
major_assignments.description: Design and build an interactive Figma prototype based on your research specification, then conduct moderated usability testing with real users. Iterate the design based on test findings and document the complete design evolution from first wireframe to tested, refined prototype.
objectives: Create wireframes and an interactive Figma prototype with at least 12 linked screens
Conduct moderated usability testing with at least 5 participants using think-aloud protocol
Analyze test results with severity-rated findings and prioritized recommendations
Iterate the design based on test findings with clear before/after documentation
deliverables: Wireframes for all key screens with annotations explaining design rationale
Interactive Figma prototype with complete user flow for the primary task
Usability test report with methodology, findings (severity-rated), and video clip evidence
Revised prototype with before/after comparisons for each design change
Design evolution document showing how the design changed from wireframe through testing to final version

criteria: Wireframes demonstrate clear information hierarchy and interaction flow
Prototype supports complete primary task flow with realistic interactions
Usability test conducted with proper protocol, neutral facilitation, and think-aloud
Design iterations directly address specific test findings with evidence-based changes
Prototype follows visual design principles: hierarchy, consistency, appropriate spacing
Interaction patterns are appropriate for the platform and task type
Before/after comparisons clearly show what changed and why
Prototype is polished enough to communicate the design concept without explanation
Test tasks are realistic and measurable with clear success criteria
Findings include both quantitative metrics (success rate, time, errors) and qualitative insights
Severity ratings are justified and consistent across findings
Iterations prioritize high-severity findings and demonstrate measurable improvement
Usability test report includes methodology sufficient for replication
Video clips support key findings with clear timestamps and annotations
Design evolution tells a compelling story from research to validated design
Remaining known issues documented with recommended next iteration priorities
Complete interactive prototype supporting primary user task flow
Rigorous usability testing with proper methodology and analysis
Evidence-based design iteration with measurable improvements
Professional documentation of the complete design evolution

major_assignments.title: Accessible, Multi-Platform Design System
major_assignments.description: Extend your design into a comprehensive, accessible design system that works across mobile, desktop, and an emerging platform (voice or AR). Build a component library with accessibility compliance, create platform-adaptive designs, and deliver a final portfolio-quality case study documenting the complete UCD process.
objectives: Build a Figma design system with reusable components, design tokens, and usage documentation
Achieve WCAG 2.1 AA compliance across all designs with documented accessibility testing
Create platform-adaptive designs for mobile, desktop, and one emerging platform (voice or AR)
Produce a portfolio case study documenting the complete user-centered design process
deliverables: Figma component library with design tokens, component variants, and usage documentation
Accessibility audit report with WCAG 2.1 AA compliance evidence and screen reader test results
Platform-adaptive designs showing how the core experience translates across mobile, desktop, and emerging platform
Portfolio case study (8-10 pages or equivalent web page) documenting the complete UCD process from research through final design

criteria: Design system includes design tokens, at least 10 reusable components with variants, and usage guidelines
All designs pass WCAG 2.1 AA automated checks with axe DevTools
Manual accessibility testing performed with keyboard and screen reader on primary task flow
Platform-adaptive designs respect each platform's conventions while maintaining brand consistency
Design system is visually coherent with consistent use of typography, color, and spacing
Components are well-designed with appropriate states (default, hover, active, disabled, error)
Platform adaptations are thoughtful, not just responsive resizing
Portfolio case study is visually polished and tells a compelling design story
Design system components are efficiently organized for designer productivity
Accessibility compliance is integrated into the system, not added after the fact
Emerging platform design addresses the unique UX challenges of that medium
Portfolio case study demonstrates clear return on investment of the UCD process
Component documentation includes do/don't examples and accessibility requirements
Accessibility audit is thorough with specific violation citations and remediation evidence
Portfolio case study includes research evidence, design rationale, test results, and iteration history
Case study honestly addresses limitations, challenges, and lessons learned
Comprehensive design system with tokens, components, and documentation
WCAG 2.1 AA compliant designs with evidence from automated and manual testing
Thoughtful platform-adaptive designs for three platforms including an emerging technology
Portfolio-quality case study documenting the complete UCD journey

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for usability testing sessions, design critiques, and card sorting exercises. These cannot be replicated individually.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late. Usability test session dates are firm.
course_policies.collaboration: Design projects are individual work. Peer feedback during design critiques is expected, encouraged, and graded. Testing each other's prototypes is required.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All designs must be original work. Using UI kits, component libraries, and Figma community files is allowed with attribution. Claiming others' research or designs as your own is plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Figma (free education plan), a screen reader (VoiceOver on macOS or NVDA on Windows), screen recording software, and access to mobile devices for prototype testing.
